intro:
Marcus is the Latin form of Mark, used in three New Testament passages: Colossians 4:10, Philemon 24, and 1 Peter 5:13. He is identified in Colossians 4:10 as the cousin of Barnabas (not "sister's son" as the KJV renders it), which clarifies the relationship — Barnabas was Mark's older cousin, not his uncle. Paul sends greetings from Marcus and gives instructions that if he comes to Colossae, the church should welcome him. Philemon 24 lists Marcus as one of Paul's fellow workers during what appears to be a Roman imprisonment. In 1 Peter 5:13 the greeting comes from "my son Mark" — suggesting a close spiritual relationship between Peter and Mark.

These references belong to the later Pauline and Petrine letters, indicating that Marcus/Mark had been fully restored to both Paul's and Peter's confidence after his earlier failure — he had departed from Paul and Barnabas at Perga during the first missionary journey (Acts 13:13), leading Paul to refuse to take him on the second journey (Acts 15:37–40), causing the split between Paul and Barnabas. The later letters show a full reconciliation. Church tradition identifies Marcus with the John Mark of Acts and with the author of the Gospel of Mark, which Papias in the early second century attributed to Peter's interpreter.
